title: The Shattered Display Case

displayCase.hover_text: The shattered glass displayCase. Overturned trays and cake crumbs are scattered everywhere.

bat_witness.hover_text: A nocturnal neighbor flapping near the window frame, looking wide-eyed at the damage.

completeDialogue: The Baker Sheep carefully folds your sorted checklist. 'Brilliant. Take this directly down to the police station. Go with Bat—he can help you pick out the matching suspect from the lineup!'
completeHint: Head to the city police office.

phases.comment: ACTION 1: Introduce Array structures and forEach looping patterns.
phases.speaker: Baker Sheep
phases.dialogue: See, look at the state of my Bakery! The thief completely destroyed my perfectly organized displayCase. I have no idea what they took! Could you make me a remainingDessert list of what's still left, while I try to clean up all this glass? It might give the police a clue to whoever did this.
phases.output:
Loop structure initialized...
→ Entering block loop mode.
→ Iterating across displayCase elements...
phases.lesson: An array is an ordered list of items, where each individual item is called an element. To look at every element inside an array in order, we use a loop block: arrayName.forEach(element => { ... }). We use the opening curly brace '{' to mark the starting line of actions we want to perform on every single element.

phases.comment: ACTION 2: Accessing object properties inside code blocks using dot notation and array.push().
phases.speaker: Bunny's Notebook
phases.dialogue: When examining an array of objects, you need to target exactly what properties you're checking for. Because array lists are numbered, inserting elements in the middle forces the computer to re-number everything else—costing lots of effort! That's why I usually append elements to the end using **array.push(newElement);** inside my looping brackets! Remember to close your action line with a **;** and seal the loop block with a **}**!
phases.output:
Cataloging sequence execution complete!
→ Name attributes extracted successfully.
→ missingDessertList populated with unorganized entries.
phases.lesson: To access specific details belonging to an object, attach a dot followed by the property name (like dessert.name). The push() action appends that specific string straight onto the end of your missingDessertList array container.
lizard_notes: UNSORTED LIST: missingDessertList contains items, but the ordering is chaotic.

phases.comment: ACTION 3: Advanced custom sorting callback method utilizing custom comparative objects.
phases.speaker: Baker Sheep
phases.dialogue: Wow. This list is a mess. A complete disaster. The messy display case has resulted in a completely disorganized mess. This is not helpful at all. Here, just this once, I'll give you access to our super secret family sorting method **.sheepOrdering()**. Please use it to reorder this missingDessertList so it's easier to read!
phases.output:
Running family sorting parameters...
→ Reordering items alphabetically and weight-matching...
→ missingDessertList is now perfectly organized! ✓
phases.lesson: Oftentimes using a basic .sort() to organize things alphabetically isn't enough or right for the task. In cases like these, you can define custom sorting rules inside the parentheses by passing two items at once to check their relationship against each other: list.sort((a, b) => a.customMethod(b));
lizard_notes: MATCHING METADATA: Discrepancies point toward heavy sugar-crust consumption patterns.

phases.comment: TRANSITION CUTSCENE: Bat witness arrives to deliver clues and point toward the police precinct.
phases.speaker: Bat Witness
phases.dialogue: Oh no, did someone break in?! I guess that is what all that crashing noise was last night... I couldn't see them very clearly from my roost. But I definitely saw that they had round ears, they were quite small, and... Oh! They squeaked!
phases.output:
LOG: Suspect profile updated
→ Clues logged: [Round Ears, Small Stature, Squeaks]
phases.lesson: Recording observations into your logs using console.log stores crucial string evidence safely before moving environments.
lizard_notes: WITNESS CLUE 2: Small Stature / Height Profile
WITNESS CLUE 3: Sound profile matches squeaking noises
